channel.title: Opensource Sunday – Sessions
channel.description: Recent content in Sessions on Opensource Sunday

item.title: Session 1A · Kickoff
item.description:
Part A of Session 1 · Covenant.

The first night. Nobody yet knows how this goes. Your job tonight is to set a tone — warm, honest, no-phones, no-dumb-questions — and to plant the word covenant in every kid’s head before they walk out.

Before class

  • Write down attendance and share it with the confirmation director
  • Pens and slips of paper ready for the closing reflection
  • Bibles for the Joshua 24 reading
  • Whiteboard or phone notes ready for the ground-rules list

Opening — Go-Around

Have everyone share (including teachers):

  • Name
  • School they attend
  • Highs and lows from the summer
  • Do you have any siblings? What order do you fall in?
    • What’s cool about that? What’s not so cool about that?

Confirmation Is a Journey

Say:

Confirmation is not meant to be like school, or a class — it’s meant to be a journey of faith together. It’s a journey where we learn and practice together what it means to be a Christian and to follow Jesus.

At the end of confirmation, if you so choose, you’ll have the opportunity to make a public profession of faith and to join the church.

It’s also a journey we take together.

Have everyone join the confirmation group chat. (See the Group Chat Setup page for the how-and-why.)

Ground Rules

We also want to create a few ground rules. Teacher, write these down in a note in your phone. (Examples: listen louder than you speak, no putdowns…)

Ask:

What would be some good ground rules?

Teachers, make sure to add:

  • Everyone opens or closes in prayer at least once
  • What happens at Confirmation stays at Confirmation — unless it’s a danger to you or someone else
  • No question is a dumb question
  • No phones

Would You Rather?

Ask:

Would you rather have to break a promise, or have a promise broken to you? Explain.

Read — Joshua 24

Say:

Today we’re going to briefly look at a passage in the Bible in the book of Joshua that deals with commitments and promises from God to God’s people, the nation of Israel. The Bible is the story of God’s people that leads us to Jesus.

Joshua was the leader of Israel after Moses. The Israelites were enslaved by Pharaoh in Egypt. Then God used Moses to lead them out of slavery, but they spent forty years wandering in the desert.

Finally they come to a new place called Canaan, but Moses dies and Joshua becomes their leader. The people in the land of Canaan follow other gods and idols — they have statues they believe are connected to other gods, and they worship those statues. Israel would fall into this same practice in the future.

But before this can happen, God uses Joshua to speak to the people about their covenant with God and how they should live.

Pass around Bibles. Choose a few readers and read:

  • Joshua 24:1–3, 13–15, 22–25

Ask:

  1. What do you think a covenant is? (v.25)
    • A covenant is a promise for the sake of relationship. God makes a covenant with us to love us and always be with us. We fulfill that covenant — that promise — by loving God and loving others.
  2. What do you think it means to “put away the foreign gods that are among you, and incline your hearts to the Lord, the God of Israel” (v.23)?
    • What are some examples of foreign gods today?
  3. What are some things we can do to be Joshua for each other and stand firm in our commitment to love God and love others throughout this confirmation journey?
  4. What promises do you think you’ll be asked to make at Confirmation?

The Big Four

Say:

Over the next several months we’ll be exploring the big four questions that you’ll be asked on the day of your confirmation in June:

  1. Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the ways of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world?
  2. Who is your Lord and Savior?
  3. Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love?
  4. Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching and fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers?

Written Reflection

Pass out pens and slips of paper. Write the following two questions on the board and have students answer them. They can — but don’t have to — write their names on the slips. Collect the slips afterwards.

  • What is one thing you want to get out of your confirmation journey?
  • What questions do you have about God?

Ask:

Is anyone open to sharing what they wrote?

Close

Say:

The next class is next week and your parents are expected to attend. (The confirmation director will email parents as well.)

Pray out together as a class.

item.title: Session 1B · Covenant
item.description:
Part B of Session 1 · Covenant.

First night with parents in the room. Names still being learned. Tonight you get the whole Confirmation year onto one page: what a covenant is, why it matters, what the three-way confirmation means, and what you’re asking of students, parents, and leaders.

Before class

  • Name tags for everyone
  • Printed list for people to write full names (students + parents; mark “B!” if the confirmand has been baptized). A template appendix is at the bottom of this page.
  • Printed Confirmation calendars for parents
  • Note cards and pens on tables
  • Tell students to sit with their family

Welcome and Prayer

Welcome to Confirmation. Let’s pray.

Ground rules (quick recap):

  • Be kind, be present (phones away)
  • Safe space
  • Everyone prays once
  • No question is a dumb question

Introductions

  • Introduce the teachers. “We’ll take turns leading, and today I’m up.”
  • Today we want to:
    • Get to know one another
    • Explore the meaning of Confirmation
    • Discuss the commitments necessary for this journey

At tables

People introduce themselves by counting off 1–4 and answering the question on the screen:

  1. Biggest animal you’ve seen in person
  2. Biggest group of people you’ve spoken in front of
  3. Longest road trip or flight
  4. Biggest decision you’ve ever made

Big group

Find out who in the room has seen the biggest animal, spoken in front of the biggest group, taken the longest trip, and made the biggest decision.

Discussion — Promises and Commitments

At tables:

  1. What are some promises and commitments you’ve made or expect to make one day? (i.e. pledge of allegiance, honor code at school, marriage, etc.)
  2. Why do we make promises?
  3. What difference does a promise make in the way we act?
  4. What happens when we break promises? Who is hurt?

Each group shares a few answers with the entire class.

Read — Covenant

Say:

The reason we’re talking about promises is because the Confirmation class journey is all about covenant.

We first see the term covenant in the Old Testament. Covenant is a legal term that binds two parties together. In the Bible, we see that God initiates the covenant with us. It’s a promise with the intent of relationship.

We see this covenant-making between God and humanity all over Scripture, but look with me to Deuteronomy 7:9.

Deuteronomy 7:9 — “Know therefore that the Lord your God is God; he is the faithful God, keeping his covenant of love to a thousand generations of those who love him and keep his commandments.”

Say:

There are two parties in this divine covenant: God and us.

Scripture describes God’s covenant with us — and we know God meant what he said because: Jesus.

Jesus coming and walking among us, teaching us how to live and to love, and then giving his life to pay the cost of our sin, freeing us from being separated from God — Jesus is the fulfillment of God’s covenant to us. God makes his covenant and fulfills his end of it, because he loves us.

We’re going to spend this Confirmation journey getting to know God through Jesus.

Who is God? Who does God say we are? And how has God called each of you to partner with him in the world?

Confirmation is a time for asking questions — so ask all of the questions you want.

The Three-Way Confirmation

Say:

So that’s God’s part — but what about us? Relationships take two. Like the text we read, it goes both ways. While God is the initiator and the ultimate fulfiller of the covenant, he invites us to respond by following him and loving his people.

Ask:

  • Who here has been baptized? Who hasn’t? (If you haven’t, you’ll have the opportunity.)
  • Does anyone know why we get baptized?

Say:

  • Individuals are received into the church to share in its life and ministry. It’s a statement of your identity as a beloved child of God — of God choosing you.
  • At baptism, the parents, the sponsors, and the congregation publicly promise to help raise these (typically) infant children into adults.
    • Those adults include Sunday School teachers, mentors at the youth ministry, and every adult in the congregation.

Say:

So other than being an example of a covenantal promise, why am I talking about baptism?

Because going through Confirmation is really a three-way confirmation:

  1. The Holy Spirit confirms our baptismal identity as a child of God.
  2. The church confirms your gifts and membership in the body of Christ.
  3. You confirm your faith in Jesus and the teachings of the church.
    • At the end of this process, this is your decision.

The Big Four (Preview)

Say:

You will be asked what we call the big four questions — questions we’ll spend the entire year talking about and looking to see what the Bible has to say about them.

So:

  • Confirmation isn’t a graduation. It might be a new stage in your faith. It might be the start of making your faith your own. But it isn’t meant to end here.
  • We want to learn, but we also want to have fun. Life with God is an adventure.

Because faith is a journey we all take together — and like any journey, it requires commitment.

Commitments of Confirmation Class

Students

  • Attendance each week. (You can miss if you’re sick, if there’s a special event, or a family gathering — but make this a priority.)
  • Be in church.
  • Engage with your parents at home.
  • Be honest.
  • No phones.

Parents

  • Thank you for driving your kids. It is exhausting to drive to practice, drive to youth group, drive to Sunday School, drive them to friends’ houses. Thank you, thank you, thank you.
  • Lead your son or daughter by example. Attend worship. Pray at meals. Model what it means to follow Jesus. Be honest about your faith, including the struggles and the doubts.
    • And attend the few parent classes on the calendar.
  • Communicate with us regularly and spend some time each week engaging in your student’s Confirmation journey. We know you have a lot going on, so we want to make that as easy as possible.

Our Commitment to You

  • We’re committed to walking with you and your family in faith.
    • The teachers will divide up the class and check in with you regularly.
    • We’re committed to wrestling alongside students as they explore their faith and ask questions.
  • We’ll send an email every other week recapping what we learned and giving you a practice for at home.

Close

Say:

I’ve done a ton of talking — but for the remainder of the year, this will be much more of a discussion. We want to be a group of fellow travelers learning more about God and one another on this faith journey.

Hand out:

  • Parent calendars
  • Bagel sign-up

Before you go: have each family write full names of parents and students, and mark with “B!” if the confirmand has been baptized.

Ask: Are there any questions?

Pray out.

Appendix — Full Name Sign-up Sheet

Print a single-sheet handout with the following header and a numbered list of 20 blank lines for families to fill in at the end of class:

Parents’ and students’ full names (including middle names)

Write a “B!” if the confirmand HAS been baptized.

item.title: Session 2A · Trust
item.description:
Part A of Session 2 · Trust.

Overview. When God calls us into covenant relationship, God calls us to trust. When we understand how God has acted in the past, is acting in the present, and promises to act tomorrow, we can trust in God’s promises even in the face of uncertainty. When we come to know God, we come to trust God.

The big question for the next two weeks: How do we know and trust God?

Two ways to run this session. Source material includes two complete variants — pick the one that fits your space and weather.

  • Outdoor (recommended): Start with a blindfolded trust walk in a park, then teach the psalm. Song (Oceans) moves to Session 2B.
  • Indoor: Skip the trust walk, go straight into discussion, and include Oceans in this session.

Both variants are laid out below. Sections marked “Outdoor only” or “Indoor only” are variant-specific; everything else is shared.

Before class

  • Prep teachers with the testimony questions (see below)
  • Note cards for questions at the end
  • Print song lyrics (Oceans by Hillsong United) — indoor variant
  • Bluetooth speaker — indoor variant
  • Blindfolds, blankets, two Bibles — outdoor variant
  • Text confirmands in advance: dress warmly, wear sneakers — outdoor variant

Icebreaker

Outdoor only — the trust walk:

Blindfolded walk through the park where first the partner guide can touch them, then only their voice, and they are spun around at various times.

Ask:

  • What was hard about that?
  • Where did trust come in?
  • How was that walk like following God?

Both variants — go-around:

  • Name
  • Where you fall in the birth order of your siblings
    • What’s cool about that?
    • What’s not so cool about that?

Indoor only — add:

  • Who do you trust most in the world? Why?
  • What does it take to build trust? To lose it?

Ground Rules

Say:

First, we want to create a circle of trust here, so we want to come up with some ground rules — a covenant we want to make with one another.

Examples:

  • No question is a dumb question
  • Everyone has to pray at least once (last one in the room has to pray)
  • Listen louder than you speak
  • What happens here stays here — unless it’s a danger to you or someone else
  • Three put-ups for a put-down
  • No cursing

Get the group chat going (if it’s not already running from Session 1 — see Group Chat Setup).

Teacher Testimony

Say:

We hope you can trust us teachers — but now we want to talk about what it looks like to trust God. First, we’ll share a little bit of our own faith journey.

Have teachers share openly and briefly on these questions:

  • Describe what it looks and feels like to know and trust God.
  • When did you have a tough time trusting God?
  • How did you come to know and trust God? (What things have helped you be close to God / know God?)

Discussion — Knowing and Trusting

Say:

Trust always comes through relationships. Just like with anyone, before we can trust God, we have to know God.

Ask:

  • Who do you know best in the world?
    • How do you know them best?
  • How can you know someone? (You spend time with them now. You look at the patterns they’ve shown in the past.)

Read — Psalm 105

Say:

The Bible is one of the ways we can know who God is — especially by looking at who God has been in the past.

The book of Psalms is a book of songs and poems used in the worship life of God’s people, from the Old Testament through today. It’s been called “a Bible within the Bible” because there’s so much in it — joy, sadness, anger, the past, the present, the future. Today we’ll look at one of the psalms that sings of the past.

Ask volunteers to read aloud from Psalm 105:

  • Abraham and Sarah — Psalm 105:7–11
  • Joseph — Psalm 105:16–22 (outdoor variant) or Psalm 105:16–25 (indoor variant)
  • Moses and the exodus — Psalm 105:37–41 (outdoor) or Psalm 105:26–36 (indoor)

Ask:

  • What does the psalm tell you about God being trustworthy? Gracious? Merciful?

Say:

Knowing how someone has typically acted in the past is helpful — but does that mean we fully know them?

We have to have relationship with them now.

(Outdoor variant:) How can we do that?

Worship — Oceans (indoor only)

Listen to the song together — “Oceans (Where Feet May Fail)” by Hillsong United. Listen for ways the author talks about trusting or growing their trust in God.

  • Listen: YouTube · Spotify · Apple Music
  • Read along: Genius lyrics
Lyric display in class. If you want to project or print the lyrics, make sure your church’s CCLI license covers it. “Oceans (Where Feet May Fail)” is copyrighted by Hillsong Music Publishing; we don’t reproduce the lyrics on this open-source site.

Ask:

  • How do you call upon the name of God?
  • How does your soul rest in God’s embrace?
  • How do we invite the Spirit to lead us? To be in the presence of our Savior?

The Promises of God

Say:

As we come to trust God, we come to be able to trust in the promises of God.

One current promise of God:

Matthew 28:20b — “And remember, I am with you always, to the end of the age.”

One future promise of God:

Revelation 21:4 — “He will wipe every tear from their eyes. Death will be no more; mourning and crying and pain will be no more, for the first things have passed away.”

Close

Say:

The journey of faith is about more and more deeply trusting God — and so it’s about more and more deeply knowing God through building relationship with him.

Close with worship. Ask a volunteer (or rotate through the circle) to read Psalm 105:1–5 one line at a time.

item.title: Session 2B · Trust
item.description:
Part B of Session 2 · Trust.

The big question continues: How do we know and trust God?

Pair this session with the variant you ran last week.

  • If you did the outdoor trust walk in 2A, include Oceans here and skip the indoor trust-walk activity.
  • If you did the indoor variant of 2A, include the indoor trust-walk activity here.

Both paths are marked below.

Opening

Ask: Someone refresh me on what I missed last week.

Ask: What is trust?

Firm belief in the reliability, truth, ability, or strength of someone or something.

Ask: What are some actual practices or ways you can build your relationship with God? Ways to know God better?

Possible answers: read the Bible, pray, show love to others, worship, listen to Christian music, obey, serve, live with gratitude, seek like-minded people, live intentionally, live with optimism.

Worship — Oceans (after outdoor variant)

If your group did the trust walk last week, this is where the Oceans song lands.

Listen to the song and read the lyrics together — “Oceans” by Hillsong United. Listen for ways the author talks about trusting or growing their trust in God. (Lyrics appendix on the Session 2A page.)

Ask:

  • How do you call upon the name of God?
  • How does your soul rest in God’s embrace?
  • How do we invite the Spirit to lead us? To be in the presence of our Savior?

Trust Walk Activity (indoor-only variant)

If your group did NOT do the outdoor trust walk last week, do this now instead.

Blindfold + walk a simple maze led by the group. Let each student take a turn being blindfolded and being led by the voice of a classmate.

Read — Hebrews 11

Say:

Let’s look at one small part of the Bible that lays down why to trust and who did.

Hebrews 11:1–38 is a list of the faithful who have trusted God “by faith.” (Faith and trust in God can be used interchangeably.)

Quick background: the book of Hebrews is a letter in the New Testament written to remind the people to remain faithful to Jesus. Two main goals: first, to show Jesus as superior — worthy of total trust and devotion; second, to remind the people to remain faithful to Jesus even through hard times.

Ask volunteers to read:

  • Noah — Hebrews 11:7 (cross-reference Genesis 6:13–22)
  • Abraham — Hebrews 11:8–9 (Genesis 12:1–8)
  • Sarah — Hebrews 11:11 (Genesis 21:1–7)
  • Abraham again — Hebrews 11:17–18 (Genesis 22:1–18)
  • Moses — Hebrews 11:23–29 (Exodus 14:21–30)
  • Rahab — Hebrews 11:31 (Joshua 2:1–14)

Ask:

  1. What does Hebrews 11 tell you about God’s people? About God in your life?
  2. Based on what you’ve found in these stories, what do you think faith — or by faith — means?
  3. Read aloud Hebrews 11:1–3. How does this help us fill out the definition of faith?

Discussion — Do You Trust God?

Ask:

  • Do you trust God? Why? Why not?
  • Can you always depend on God?
  • What about when sad things happen? Is God still being dependable then?
  • Why is it important for confirmands to trust that God is gracious and merciful?

Continuum exercise (after-outdoor variant):

Have students stand in the middle of the room. Read each statement — students move to one side if they agree, the other if they disagree, or stay in the middle if they’re unsure.

  • God is always with you.
  • God has a good plan for you.
  • God always hears your prayers.
  • When bad things happen, you trust it is always going to work out.

Get Up and Move — Would You Rather?

Stand in the middle of the room. Ask each question; students move to a side of the room based on their answer.

  1. Would you rather eat sushi ten days past its expiration date, or chew pre-chewed gum found on a New York City subway?
  2. Would you rather drink a tablespoon of pond water, or a ten-year-old bottle of expired soda?
  3. Would you rather eat sardines every day for a week, or always use the same fork, spoon, and knife used by every person in a local restaurant — without being washed?
  4. Would you rather cross an ocean in an inflatable raft, or fly across the continent in an airplane that had been in a crash?
  5. Would you rather have ten arms or ten legs?

Now come back and sit down.

Ask:

  • Why would it be hard to trust eating, drinking, or traveling in these things?
  • Why do these choices gross us out or make us nervous when we think about them?
  • What are some reasons we wouldn’t trust eating, drinking, or using these items?

Share these concepts:

  • When we believe something will make us sick, or that something is unreliable to use, we cannot trust it. We want to avoid those items at all costs.
  • Things in this world will fall apart or go bad, and we have a hard time trusting something that we think could cause us harm or put us in a difficult situation. (And the last question — I was really just curious what you’d answer.)
  • Only God is completely trustworthy. He deserves our trust and confidence. And as we come to know him, we will come to trust him.

Video — Eagles Coach on Trusting God

Play the short “Eagles coach about trusting God” clip from your files. (Original source: ~1–2 minutes of an NFL head-coach post-game interview about his trust in God.)

Close

Close in prayer.

item.title: Session 3A · Sin
item.description:
Part A of Session 3 · Sin.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules posted; collect phones
  • Target + something soft to throw at it (ping-pong balls, rolled socks, a small ball)
  • Screen or laptop ready for the BibleProject video
  • Bibles for all

Opening

Ask:

  • What are the sort of things that break relationships?
  • When you hear the word sin, what comes to mind?

Activity — Missing the Mark

Have students take turns throwing the object at a difficult target — far enough away that most of them will miss.

Ask:

  • Was it difficult to hit the mark? Why?
  • What if you were to practice?
  • What if you had a coach or a guide?

Video — BibleProject on Sin (Khata)

Play the BibleProject short video on the Hebrew word for sin, khata (to miss the mark): https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/khata-sin/

Ask:

  • Did anything jump out to you about the video?
  • What do you think it means that every hero of the Bible sinned — except Jesus?
    • It means God expects us to miss the mark, and loves us anyway.
    • God uses imperfect people to do extraordinary things.
    • Forgiveness is always available.

Read — Matthew 22:34–40

Matthew 22:34–40 — Hearing that Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, the Pharisees got together. One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with this question: “Teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the Law?”

Jesus replied: “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.’ This is the first and greatest commandment. And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’ All the Law and the Prophets hang on these two commandments.”

Ask:

  • Who is in the passage?
  • Who can give a summary of what happened?
  • What relationships do the greatest commandments talk about?

Draw or reference a cross: there’s our vertical relationship with God and our horizontal relationship with people.

Say:

Sin always means broken relationships. We miss the mark when we don’t love God and neighbor.

Ask: Does anyone do these things perfectly? No — we all miss the mark.

What Causes Sin?

Read — Genesis 3:1–6

Genesis 3:1–6 — Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. He said to the woman, “Did God really say, ‘You must not eat from any tree in the garden’?”

The woman said to the serpent, “We may eat fruit from the trees in the garden, but God did say, ‘You must not eat fruit from the tree that is in the middle of the garden, and you must not touch it, or you will die.’”

“You will not certainly die,” the serpent said to the woman. “For God knows that when you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.”

When the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it.

Little-s sin vs. Big-S Sin

Say:

There are the little-s sins we commit and the big-S Sin that is a power — a power that causes sins and separation from God.

The Bible talks about big-S Sin as caused by three things:

  1. Satan — symbolized by the snake in many traditions. Supernatural powers of evil that try to lie and distort things.
  2. Flesh — our own misled desires or even thoughts.
  3. The World — the influence of others around us.

We’ll continue with our response to sin next week.

item.title: Session 3B · Sin
item.description:
Part B of Session 3 · Sin.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; collect phones
  • Bibles for all
  • Notebooks (if using the Pg 23 / Pg 27 resources) or printed equivalents

Recap

Last week we said that sin is missing the mark. Missing the mark at what? Loving God and loving others.

Read — Exodus 20:1–17 (The Ten Commandments)

Open Bibles and read Exodus 20:1–17 together — the Ten Commandments. These are God’s specific articulation of what it looks like to love God and love neighbor.

The Sources of Sin (quick recap)

Little-s sins and big-S Sin, caused by:

  • Satan
  • Flesh
  • The World — the influence of others around us

Does God Punish Us for Our Sins?

Say:

In the Old Testament, we see that God punishes the people of Israel for their sins — they break the law and receive the consequence. And yet we see that no matter how badly they miss the mark, God always welcomes them back.

In his infinite wisdom and grace, God sends his son Jesus to take on the penalty for our sins once and for all.

Jesus and the New Testament teach us not that we are necessarily punished for our sins — but that our sin separates us from God, the God who is the only one who provides abundant life.

We are also taught that God does not bless sin, and that in life after death we will be held accountable. The good news is that the one who will hold us accountable is the same one who gave his life for ours.

The Bible always takes sin seriously, because it destroys life. Out of a response to that great love, we are called to confront sin and evil in our own lives and in the world.

Read — Matthew 4:17

Matthew 4:17 — From that time Jesus began to proclaim, “Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.”

Ask:

  • What does repent mean?

Say:

The Greek word is metanoia — a change of mind, a turn in direction. It doesn’t mean just feeling bad about what you did. It means actually turning around.

Our Response to Sin

In our own lives

Ask:

  • What might a faithful response to sin be in our own lives?
    • Confession. Making amends.

(Optional resource: Pg 23 in the confirmation notebooks — take turns reading a verse.)

In the world

Ask: What might be a faithful response to sin in the world?

(Optional resource: Pg 27 in the confirmation notebooks — for each listed sin, name the faithful response.)

Discussion

Ask:

  1. Is it a sin to lie? Why?
  2. Is it a sin to not help someone who falls and hurts themselves? Why?
  3. Is it a sin if everyone pollutes the lake and the drinking water gets contaminated? Why?
  4. Is it a sin if there is a law that prevents people with the name Bob from getting into college? Why?

Close

Say:

We all miss the mark, and there’s a consequence to our sin. Jesus takes those consequences onto himself and offers us forgiveness — and sends us out to bring life and repair.

As we spend time with Jesus and his people, it becomes easier and easier to follow him.

The first of the Big Four vows — this is what we’ve been building toward:

Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the way of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world?

Close in prayer.

item.title: Session 4A · Lord
item.description:
Part A of Session 4 · Lord.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; collect phones
  • One piece of paper for each table
  • Pens for each table
  • Slides
  • Cut out the Bible passages in the appendix (one set per competing team)

Opening — At Tables

  • Who is your favorite sports team? (If you don’t have one: favorite artist.)
    • Why are they your favorite?
  • What would it take to make you switch your loyalty to their biggest rival?
  • What if you moved to a different state — would you change your loyalties? Why or why not?

Big Group — What Does “Lord” Mean?

Recap the table answers.

Ask: What does Lord mean?

Someone or something having power, authority, or influence; a master or ruler.

Big question for the next two weeks: Who is your Lord?

The next two lessons are about where our ultimate loyalties lie — and how that impacts the way we live.

Read — Exodus 3:13–15 (The Burning Bush)

Have people take turns reading with emotional inflections: narrator, Moses, God.

Introduce the reading:

Moses has fled Egypt and is living in Midian. He’s married, looking after the flocks of his father-in-law, Jethro. In search of fresh pasture, Moses arrives at Mount Horeb — “the mountain of God.” In this holy place, God speaks to him through a burning bush that will not be consumed by fire. In this encounter, God’s name is revealed to Moses.

Exodus 3:13–15 — Moses said to God, “Suppose I go to the Israelites and say to them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you,’ and they ask me, ‘What is his name?’ Then what shall I tell them?”

God said to Moses, “I am who I am. This is what you are to say to the Israelites: ‘I am has sent me to you.’”

God also said to Moses, “Say to the Israelites, ‘The Lord, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — has sent me to you.’ This is my name forever, the name you shall call me from generation to generation.”

Ask:

  • What is God’s name according to v.14? (In Hebrew this is Yahweh*.)*
  • What do you think this name means?

Say:

“I AM” is the ultimate statement of self-sufficiency and being ever-present. God is able to do what he wills to do and accomplish what he wills to accomplish. He is utterly dependable, present, and powerful. In other words: God is the Lord of all.

Competition — Jesus as Lord Is ______

Say:

We know God is the all-powerful Lord. But just what kind of Lord is he? Well — Jesus shows us.

Split into groups and divvy up the Bible passages from the appendix. Each group reads their passages together.

Say:

This is a competition. We’re going to go from table to table asking you to fill in this statement: “Jesus as Lord is ______.” You have about five minutes to look at the scriptures and come up with as many answers as you can. Write them down.

After five minutes, go in turn to each table — no repeats. Winner is the last one standing.

Read together to close:

John 10:10 — “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.”

Say: This is the kind of Lord that Jesus is.

The Other Lords

Say:

The reality is: it’s not if we’ll have a Lord. It’s who or what will be our Lord. There are many things that try to have authority, power, and influence over us — that try to claim the ultimate lordship over our lives. Some aren’t necessarily bad; some are more sinister. But none of them deserve the number-one spot.

The Scriptures name and warn us about these things often. Here’s an example from the Apostle Paul’s letter to the church in Philippi:

Philippians 3:19 — “Their end is destruction, their god is the belly, and their glory is in their shame; their minds are set on earthly things.”

Ask at tables: What other things might try to have lordship over our lives? (Remember: Lord = someone or something having power, authority, or influence; a master or ruler.)

(Possible answers: grades, work, productivity, power, money, possessions, popularity, social media, video games, sports, relationships, friends, food.)

Say:

Compare these with the descriptions you came up with for Jesus as Lord. Notice the contrast — but don’t knock those things entirely.

Jesus as Lord is full of grace and truth, holy, seeks to do justice, cares for the poor, is forgiving, compassionate, powerful, healing, loving, wise, humble, has all authority, desires to give us rest, and immeasurably more.

Those other things aren’t necessarily all bad. But only when we give Jesus the top spot do we experience the fullest life there is.

John 10:10 — “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.”

Say:

Being a follower of Jesus means declaring the bold statement that Jesus is Lord of our lives. Next week we’ll discuss what this means for how we live.

Family Challenge

Before we pray out, a family challenge:

Choose one thing you’ll do over the next week to declare that Jesus is Lord of your family. Examples: pray before bed or a meal; set aside the phone or TV for an hour to pray and spend quality time with family; go to church together; read the Bible together (maybe Exodus 3?!); sing a worship song together; read a Psalm together in the morning.

If your family is here, work with them. If not, think about it — and take it home to them.

Prayer — Turning Out

Get everyone in a circle. Cross your right arm over your left and take the hand of the person on either side.

Ask each person to say a word that describes Jesus Christ.

Then pray:

Lord Jesus, you are all of these and more. Help us to follow your ways more closely as we leave this place. Amen.

Ask participants to keep their hands joined as they raise their right arms and move under them — creating a circle of joined hands with everyone now facing outward.

Say to the group:

When we turn to Jesus, we turn to the world he loves. Share the good news.

Appendix — Scripture Passages for the Competition

Cut these apart and distribute to groups.

  • John 1:14 — And the Word became flesh, and dwelt among us, and we saw His glory, glory as of the only begotten from the Father, full of grace and truth.
  • Luke 1:35 — The angel answered and said to her, “The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; and for that reason the holy Child shall be called the Son of God.”
  • John 5:30 — “I can do nothing on My own initiative. As I hear, I judge; and My judgment is just, because I do not seek My own will, but the will of Him who sent Me.”
  • Luke 23:34 — But Jesus was saying, “Father, forgive them; for they do not know what they are doing.” And they cast lots, dividing up His garments among themselves.
  • John 15:13 — Greater love has no one than this, that one lay down his life for his friends.
  • Matthew 9:36 — When he saw the crowds, he had compassion on them because they were confused and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd.
  • John 10:10 — I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.
  • Matthew 8:1–3 — When Jesus came down from the mountainside, large crowds followed him. A man with leprosy came and knelt before him and said, “Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean.” Jesus reached out his hand and touched the man. “I am willing,” he said. “Be clean!” Immediately he was cleansed of his leprosy.
  • Matthew 22:36–40 — “Teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the Law?” Jesus replied: “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.’ This is the first and greatest commandment. And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’ All the Law and the Prophets hang on these two commandments.”
  • Mark 10:45 — For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.
  • John 6:35 — Then Jesus declared, “I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty.”
  • Matthew 28:18 — Then Jesus came to them and said, “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.”
  • Matthew 11:28 — “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.”

Alternate Track — Elijah on Mount Carmel

Source material includes an alternate / extended passage for the which Lord will you follow? question: 1 Kings 18:20–39 (Elijah vs. the prophets of Baal).

Introduce the reading:

King Ahab was the worst king of Israel. He and his wife, Jezebel, imposed the worship of Baal (Baal means “lord”) on the people. Baal was a Canaanite nature god. The question for the people of God: which lord would they serve — Baal or YHWH? The story continues as King Ahab brought 450 prophets of Baal against Elijah, the one prophet of YHWH. It was an epic showdown. This wasn’t some typical disagreement over religion — King Ahab was actually killing off the prophets of God, essentially forcing people to worship Baal.

Have people take turns reading 1 Kings 18:20–39 with emotional inflections: Elijah, prophets of Baal, the crowd, the narrator.

Ask:

  • Listen for what you think is the most important word / phrase / image / object / event in the passage. We’ll explain our choices afterward.
  • Why was it so important for the people to decide which Lord to follow?
    • Look back at the first commandment — Exodus 20:2–3 — “I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery. You shall have no other gods before me.”
    • Our lives are defined by our Lord.

item.title: Session 4B · Lord
item.description:
Part B of Session 4 · Lord.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; collect phones
  • Whiteboard + dry-erase markers
  • Note cards, pens
  • Print and cut out the four scenarios from the appendix
  • Electronic way to show a picture (smartphone / tablet / laptop)
  • Optional: Books of the New Testament infographic

Write “Who is your Lord?” on the whiteboard.

Game — Jesus as Lord Is _______

Go around the room. Each person finishes the sentence: Jesus as Lord is ______. Whoever is the last one standing wins a prize.

Sample fills: Jesus as Lord is full of grace and truth, holy, just, working with God, forgiving, compassionate, powerful, healing, loving, wise, a servant, has authority, desires to give us rest, wants to give us abundant life — and immeasurably more.

The winner gets the opportunity to pray for the class.

Read — Philippians 2:1–13

Big question (recap): Who is your Lord? This lesson is about how the answer impacts how we live.

Introduce the reading:

(Optional: post the Books of the New Testament infographic and ask a volunteer to find “Philippians” and note who wrote it.)

The letter to the church in Philippi was written by the Apostle Paul about 20 to 30 years after Jesus lived, and it contains an early hymn to Jesus as Lord.

In Rome, the emperors were considered to be gods. Romans required all the peoples they conquered not only to honor their emperor but also to worship him as a god and call him “Lord.” That was a line neither Jews nor Christians were willing to cross. They would not call Caesar “Lord,” and they would not bow down to worship him.

Show the picture at https://bit.ly/2jTWojp. These ruins are what is left of a fountain in the city of Ephesus. The fountain was built in the second century to honor Roman Emperor Trajan. It included a huge statue of Trajan. These words were inscribed on it: “I am the ruler of the world. The world is under my foot.” Ironically, today only Trajan’s foot and a globe remain.

(If you have the photo of your leader visiting Ephesus on hand, show it here for a laugh.)

Say:

There is only one ruler of the world.

Open Bibles and get three volunteers to read Philippians 2:1–13 — verses 1–4, 5–11, 12–13.

Philippians 2:5–11 — In your relationships with one another, have the same mindset as Christ Jesus: who, being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage; rather, he made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross!

Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.

Ask:

  1. Given that the Romans demanded that all people call the emperor “Lord,” what kind of fallout would come to Christians for calling Jesus “Lord”?
    • (Persecuted. Laughed at. Not included in social circles. People thinking they were weird. Not getting jobs or opportunities.)
  2. If they were going to face these consequences, why would they call Jesus their Lord?
    • (Because he brings them abundant life.)
  3. If Jesus is Lord, how is he different from so many rulers of the world?
    • (Hint: v.5–11. He doesn’t use his power for his own gain but for the gain of others.)
  4. Should honoring Jesus as Lord — the highest authority of our lives — make a difference in how we act or what we say?

Scenarios

Break students into four groups of 3–4. Each group gets one scenario. Have them come up with two responses — one if Jesus is Lord, one if Jesus is not — and act them out after a few minutes.

Scenarios:

  1. Someone is talking badly about someone else in the locker room.
  2. The person next to you in class offers to give you the answers to the quiz.
  3. There are dishes in the sink when you get home from school.
  4. You are invited to go to the soup kitchen to feed people who are homeless Saturday morning.

The Mind of Christ

Ask for a volunteer who can draw. On the whiteboard, draw a large outline of a human head — large enough to fill the board.

Call on the youth to draw inside the head any symbols, words, or images that signify having the same mind as Christ. These could be Jesus’ attitudes, actions, or characteristics — things Paul calls Christians to imitate.

Ask: Who is someone you know that has the mindset of Christ? Why?

Pass out note cards and pens. Have students consider which particular qualities they would like to practice in their own lives and write them down.

Ask:

  • Would anyone share some of the qualities you wrote down?
  • Is it easy to live up to these qualities?
    • Accountability is helping intentions become reality. What are some ways we can hold each other accountable?
  • What are some ways we can celebrate when we see one another having the mind of Christ?

The Big Question

Say:

The big question this unit is “Who is your Lord?” You don’t have to answer out loud — but sit with these two questions:

  • Are you ready to claim Jesus Christ as your Lord? Why or why not?
  • Are you ready to live in a way that shows loyalty to your Lord? Why or why not?

Optional — Rap It Out

If there’s time: make a rap song about Jesus being Lord. Have partners choose one characteristic of Christ and make two rhyming lines. Go around and say the rap.

Close

Close in prayer.

Appendix — Scenarios for Print-and-Cut

  1. Someone is talking badly about someone else in the locker room.
  2. The person next to you in class offers to give you the answers to the quiz.
  3. There are dishes in the sink when you get home from school.
  4. You are invited to go to the soup kitchen to feed people who are homeless Saturday morning.

item.title: Session 5A · Salvation
item.description:
Part A of Session 5 · Salvation.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; ask who will open in prayer next week
  • Whiteboard + markers
  • Phones or laptops for the Blooket game
  • A guest lined up to share their salvation testimony (about 10 minutes)

Icebreaker

Ask:

  • What is your favorite thing about Thanksgiving?
  • Do you have any family Thanksgiving traditions?
  • What is your least favorite thing about Thanksgiving?

The Big Question

Write on the board: “Who is your Savior?”

These next two weeks, we’re talking about this question.

What Is a Savior?

Have students google the word Savior.

You’re likely to see two definitions:

  1. A person who saves someone or something (especially a country or cause) from danger, and who is regarded with the veneration of a religious figure.
    • Synonyms: rescuer, liberator, deliverer, emancipator, champion, knight in shining armor, friend in need, good Samaritan, salvation.
  2. (In Christianity) God or Jesus Christ as the redeemer of sin and saver of souls.
    • Synonyms: Christ, Jesus, Jesus Christ, Redeemer, Messiah, Lord.

Ask: So — what do we need to be saved from?

Game — Blooket

Play a Blooket set on “things that claim to save us.” Ours is here (use the code when prompted):

  • https://dashboard.blooket.com/set/655bb11da92a368a39ff7f5a

Ask:

  • Look at the advertisements, products, and institutions on the board. Why do these places look for problems people have and make promises to fix them?

The Presbyterian Promise — GRACE

Ask: In the Presbyterian religion, what is our promise?

Say:

Our promise is GRACE. Grace fully known in the love and sacrifice of Jesus.

Write this on the board:

G od’s R iches A t C hrist’s E xpense

Say:

Presbyterians believe that God promises a solution to our brokenness and the brokenness of the world in the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. We call that salvation.

Salvation is God’s gift — to save us from all that is against God, and to give us abundant life here and now, and in eternity.

Testimony

Introduce the guest who will share their salvation story. After they finish, open it up for questions.

Close

Close with a popcorn prayer — each person prays one short sentence or word out loud, popping up as they feel led.

item.title: Session 5B · Salvation
item.description:
Part B of Session 5 · Salvation.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; ask who will open in prayer next week
  • Bibles for all
  • Whiteboard + markers
  • Screen / laptop for YouTube videos
  • Index cards with the three passage groups written on them (see Breakout Groups below)

Opening

Ask:

  • How was everyone’s Christmas?
  • What is one Christmas tradition your family has?

Recap

Ask: What does Savior mean? (Invite synonyms; write them on the board.)

Write on the board: “Who is your Savior?”

Say:

The things of the world all promise to give us a solution to different problems.

Ask: In the Presbyterian religion, what is our promise? (Wait for answers.)

Say:

Our promise is GRACE. Grace fully known in the love and sacrifice of Jesus.

Write on the board:

God’s Riches At Christ’s Expense

Say:

Presbyterians believe that God promises a solution to our brokenness and the brokenness of the world in the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. We call that salvation. Salvation is God’s gift to save us from all that is against God and give us abundant life here and now, and in eternity.

Video

Watch one or both of the following and ask afterward: “How would you say salvation is described in that video?”

  • https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e-71IaDiUkY
  • https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fADMgCVOSvs

Salvation Is Bigger Than Heaven and Hell

Say:

Considering our eternal destiny is only one aspect of salvation. The Bible says a wide variety of things about that subject, but we can break it down into three:

  1. The Bible says that God chooses those who are saved.
  2. The Bible says that when we choose God, we are saved.
  3. The Bible says that all will be saved.

Say:

Those things live in tension — and we are called to embrace the mystery.

What we know for sure is that salvation comes through Christ, and that we experience salvation through our relationship with him. But the idea of salvation in the Bible is bigger and broader than just thinking about heaven or hell.

Read — Ephesians 2

Hand out Bibles. Have the confirmands turn to Ephesians 2.

Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 1–3.

Ask:

  • Why does Paul tell readers they were dead?
  • What kind of death is Paul talking about?

Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 4–7.

Ask:

  • How does God change people in Christ, and why?
    • Out of love, God saves people, makes them alive with Christ, and restores relationship with God through Christ.

Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 8–10.

Ask:

  • What do humans need to do to receive God’s saving love?
  • Based on these verses, what do you think grace means?
  • If our good works cannot save us, why do them?

Breakout Groups — Salvation in Three Keys

Break into groups. Give each group an index card with a salvation theme + passages and the question set. Each group reads, discusses, and presents.

Group 1 — Salvation means freedom

Read Exodus 3:1–10:

  • What did Moses and the Hebrew people need to be saved (freed) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Read Luke 19:1–10 (Zacchaeus):

  • What did Zacchaeus need to be saved (freed) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Group 2 — Salvation means healing and wholeness

Read 2 Kings 5:1–15 (Naaman):

  • What did Naaman need to be saved (healed) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Read Mark 2:1–12 (the man lowered through the roof):

  • What did the man who was paralyzed need to be saved (healed) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Group 3 — Salvation means forgiveness

Read Matthew 18:21–35 (the unmerciful servant):

  • What did the men who were in debt need to be saved (forgiven) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Read Luke 15:11–32 (the prodigal son):

  • What did the prodigal need to be saved (forgiven) from?
  • In what ways is this a story of salvation?
  • What does the story tell us about the heart of God?

Common Thread

After groups present, discuss: what’s the common theme across all three keys?

Ask: What questions do you still have about salvation?

Optional — Humanity and Divinity of Christ

If there’s time: get into a discussion about Christ’s humanity and Christ’s divinity.

Draw a continuum on the board. Ask confirmands to name things that fall on either side.

Say:

As fully human, Christ gets us and understands our struggle. As fully God, Christ has the power to save us from sin in the world.

Close

Finish with prayer.

item.title: Session 6A · Disciple
item.description:
Part A of Session 6 · Disciple.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules
  • Bibles for all
  • Things to make a cross with — sticks, twine, popsicle sticks, whatever’s on hand
  • A downstairs / outdoor space to move to for the cross-making activity

Big question for the next two weeks: What does it mean to be a disciple of Jesus?

Opening

Ask: Would you say that being a Christian is safe or risky? Why?

(Teacher: give a personal story about a time your faith cost you something.)

Ask: Sometimes we confuse belief and discipleship. Does anyone know the difference?

Say:

Belief implies something inward. Discipleship implies something active — a change in one’s daily life.

Ask: What is the central symbol of Christianity?

(The cross.)

Say:

Following Jesus is not an easy thing. It means taking up the cross.

Read — Mark 8:31–38

Introduce the reading:

In Jesus’ time, people expected God to send a Messiah — Christ, “anointed one.” Most people assumed the Messiah would lead the people of Israel in a nationalistic revolution against Rome. Peter thought Jesus would be that kind of Messiah. So when Jesus says that he must suffer, be rejected and killed, and after three days rise again — Peter reacts.

Ask a volunteer to read aloud Mark 8:31–33.

Ask:

  • Why do you think Peter rebukes (reprimands, criticizes) Jesus?
  • Why does Jesus tell Peter to get behind him?
    • Disciples follow Jesus and his teachings — not the other way around.

Ask another volunteer to read aloud Mark 8:34–38.

Ask:

  • What do you think Jesus means by “take up their cross” (v.34)?
    • Disciples should be prepared to face sacrifice and challenges like Jesus’ own life and death — when their beliefs and values conflict with those of worldly authorities.
  • How do you think Jesus’ disciples reacted to his call to take up their cross?
  • What does it look like to lose one’s life for Jesus’ sake and for the sake of the gospel (v.35)?

Cross Activity

Move downstairs (or outside). Make crosses together. As you work, consider Jesus’ call to take up your own cross — to lay down your life for the sake of others.

Ask:

  • What were you thinking of as you constructed your cross?
  • How did you feel — how do you feel — about the call to discipleship?

item.title: Session 6B · Disciple
item.description:
Part B of Session 6 · Disciple.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules
  • Church bulletins (one per student)
  • Note cards, pens
  • Whiteboard + markers

Big question continues: What does it mean to be a disciple of Jesus?

Recap

Ask: Who remembers what it means to be a disciple?

To take up your cross and follow Jesus. To make sacrifices for the sake of God and others.

Read — Acts 7:51–60 (Stephen, the First Martyr)

Say:

Acts is a history of the early church, written by Luke. Stephen is the first martyr — he stood up for his faith regardless of the consequences.

Ask a volunteer to read aloud Acts 7:51–60.

Ask:

  • Are there ways in which you stand up for your faith at home or at school?
  • What is difficult about standing up for your faith?
  • What does this story tell us about discipleship?

Say:

Following Jesus is rewarding — but it is also risky. Christ doesn’t call us to play it safe. He calls us to sacrifice and to stand up for our faith.

Ask:

  • How do you think Christian discipleship (denying self and taking up the cross) compares to loving God, neighbors, and self with mind, soul, and heart — in other words, with everything you have? How are they different?

Notecards — Courage to Stand Firm

Pass out note cards and pens. Ask confirmands to write down two situations in which it would take courage to stand firm in their faith and stay true to Jesus’ teachings.

Write these possibilities on the board to get them thinking:

  • Seeing someone being bullied
  • Being with friends who are gossiping about others
  • Being around someone who says people who believe in God are dumb
  • Choosing to honor commitments at church when others are not
  • Observing family members wasting resources or doing something harmful
  • Seeing a friend who is a Christian being hateful toward someone else

Ask:

  • What do you risk sacrificing by showing courage and standing firm?
  • What good things might happen if you do?

Read — Mark 12:41–44 (The Generous Widow)

Ask a volunteer to read aloud.

Ask:

  • Why was the woman’s gift worth more than all those who put in a lot more money than she did?
  • What does this story tell us about discipleship?

Read — Matthew 20:20–28 (The Greatest in the Kingdom)

Ask a volunteer to read aloud.

Ask:

  • What kind of kingdom do you think the mother of John and James thought Jesus was going to have?
  • Why do you think the others were angry?
  • What does this story tell us about discipleship?

Worship as the Gymnasium of Discipleship

Hand out copies of your church’s worship service bulletin. Write the components on the board.

Say:

Sunday worship is like a gymnasium — it’s meant to train us to be disciples of Christ throughout the week.

Ask:

  • What is your favorite part?
  • Which parts of the service require responses from us?
    • Where does the worship service call on church members to deny themselves? To give sacrificially?
  • What, if anything, would you change in our worship so that sacrificially following Jesus was emphasized more?

Looking Ahead — Your Own Confirmation

Say:

We follow Jesus together. Being a disciple of Jesus is about training together in the sacrificial love of Jesus. As we look ahead to confirmation:

Ask:

  • What sacrifices do you think you might be asked to make because you are being confirmed?

Commitment — I Will Follow Jesus

Write the following phrase on the board:

“I will follow Jesus. Even though following him might put me at risk, I want to stand up for ______________.”

Look back at your notecard and choose a situation (or make a new one) where you want to stand for the right thing.

One at a time, invite students to stand and say: “I will follow Jesus. Even though following him might put me at risk, I want to stand up for ____________” — naming a person, cause, or ministry.

Prompt the group’s response: “I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me.” (Philippians 4:13)

(Teacher: be prepared to stand and speak first to prompt others.)

When everyone is standing, join hands as the church and pray together for the strength to follow Jesus in your daily lives.

Tell confirmands you will pray for them this week and invite them to pray for you.

item.title: Session 7A · Love
item.description:
Part A of Session 7 · Love.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; invite someone to pray
  • Print copies of Resource 7a (the phileo vs. agape sheet) — enough for everyone or partners
  • Print copies of John 21:15–19 (narrator / Jesus / Peter roles)
  • Mural paper or large whiteboard + markers

Big question for the next two weeks:

Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love?

Opener

Ask: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?

Brainstorm synonyms for the word love — affection, adore, fondness, tenderness, devotion. Push the group to think more tangibly about acts and experiences of love.

Optional game: Last LOVE standing — go around the room; each person names a kind of love without repeating. Last one standing wins.

Phileo vs. Agape

Hand out copies of Resource 7a. Review the differences between phileo (brotherly love, mutual affection) and agape (sacrificial love). Work on the sheet together, helping one another translate the words for love in each Bible passage.

Return to the brainstormed love list on the mural paper. Ask participants to choose the synonyms and expressions of love that most closely resemble phileo and agape.

Read — John 21:15–19

Context: This is after the resurrection. Jesus has appeared to the disciples on the beach. Peter — the one who denied Jesus three times before the crucifixion — is about to be asked a question three times.

Invite volunteers to read the parts of Jesus, Peter, and a narrator.

John 21:15–19 — When they had finished eating, Jesus said to Simon Peter, “Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?”

“Yes, Lord,” he said, “you know that I love you.”

Jesus said, “Feed my lambs.”

Again Jesus said, “Simon son of John, do you love me?”

He answered, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.”

Jesus said, “Take care of my sheep.”

The third time he said to him, “Simon son of John, do you love me?”

Peter was hurt because Jesus asked him the third time, “Do you love me?” He said, “Lord, you know all things; you know that I love you.”

Jesus said, “Feed my sheep. Very truly I tell you, when you were younger you dressed yourself and went where you wanted; but when you are old you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go.” (Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God.) Then he said to him, “Follow me!”

Ask — at tables

  • Why do you think Jesus asked Simon Peter if he loved him?
  • Jesus asked Peter if he loved (agape) him. Why do you think Peter responded, “I love (phileo) you”?
  • If this were the only Bible story we had about love, how would you define Christian love?

Ask — in big group

  • What is the connection between loving Jesus and feeding his sheep?
  • Whom do sheep represent in the story?
  • Who are Jesus’ sheep today?
  • What actions constitute “feeding” them?

Application — Four Ways to Love

At tables, have each person name:

  1. One way you can show phileo at home.
  2. One way you can show phileo outside of the home (at work, school, somewhere else).
  3. One way you can show agape at home.
  4. One way you can show agape outside of the home.

Close — We Love Because He First Loved Us

1 John 4:19 — “We love because he first loved us.”

Encourage the confirmands to think of ways they are both sheep and feeders — so they see themselves as contributors as well as recipients.

Close in prayer.

item.title: Session 7B · Love
item.description:
Part B of Session 7 · Love.

Get right to it — the campus tour takes longer than you think.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; invite someone to pray
  • Let the mission-room / food-pantry leaders know you’re coming
  • Optional: coordinate an activity at the food pantry (making food bags, etc.) if your church has one
  • Invite a church member with a compelling service-and-faith story to speak for 5–10 minutes

Big question: Will you show Christ’s love?

Recap

Ask: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?

Ask: What two types of love showed up in last week’s passage?

Phileo and agape.

Ask: What’s the difference between the two?

Recap John 21:15–19.

Ask: When are you a sheep? When are you a feeder?

Walking Tour — How the Church Shows Christ’s Love

Ask: What are the different ways this church shows Christ’s love?

Then take a field trip through the building. Walk the whole campus. At each stop, name what happens there and how it’s an expression of Christ’s love. A template tour (adapt to your church):

  • Prayer pit / chapel — it all starts with prayer
  • Mission room — mission trips, water walks, partnerships; walk through the things on the walls
  • Music / arts room — music, gifts
  • Kitchen — meals, delivering meals to shut-ins
  • Fellowship hall — giving, youth group
  • Band closet / supply room — food bags for homeless-service partners, band
  • “Man cave” / utility room — mission shoes for Guatemala, middle-school missions, senior care, buildings and grounds
  • Preschool / nursery — Sunday school, preschool
  • Food Pantry — service days (Monday & Wednesday, in our case)
  • Pastoral care / connections office — getting people connected, the good-neighbor fund, volunteer coordination
  • Conference room — committees (admin, HR), prayer meetings, elders, deacons
  • Sanctuary — music, preaching, tech, Ash Wednesday, communion, tithing, weddings, funerals
  • Graveyard — care for those who have passed away

Bonus: If Ash Wednesday is coming up, ask who can help with set up and who can read the confession.

Activity — Food Pantry / Food Bags

Head to the food pantry and put together food bags.

Invite a leader to share briefly:

  • How did you get involved?
  • What do you do there?
  • How has it impacted your faith and the way you show Christ’s love?
  • How could we get involved?

Discussion — Faithful Disciple, Showing His Love

“Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, … showing his love?”

Ask:

  • How does doing the work of love help you understand who Jesus Christ is for you?
  • Why do you think sacrificial love (agape) is important for following Jesus and showing his love?
  • Why do you think mutual affection (phileo) is important for following Jesus and showing his love?
  • How will you show agape and phileo to others as a part of the church?

Close

Have students come up with their own ways to love this week — one specific thing, one specific person. Bless them in prayer.

item.title: Session 8A · Teaching
item.description:
Part A of Session 8 · Teaching.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules
  • Note cards + pens
  • Printed Resource 8a (Common English Bible + The Message paraphrases of 2 Timothy 1:9–10) — enough for everyone or partners
  • Bibles
  • The BIG Journal (or equivalent) if your program uses one; page references below assume it

Write the big question on the board:

WILL YOU DEVOTE YOURSELF TO THE CHURCH’S TEACHING?

Opener

Confirmation ground rules + highs and lows go-around.

Say:

Today we’re going to look at the Apostles’ Creed — a statement of faith that is over a thousand years old, unites multiple denominations of Christianity, and is based in what was professed by those in the early church.

I Believe / I Love

(Follow page 48 in the Teacher’s Guide.)

Direct each person (or pair) to write the words “I believe” at the top of their note card. Have them write four things:

  1. One thing they believe about God
  2. One thing they believe about Jesus
  3. One thing they believe about the Holy Spirit
  4. One thing they believe about the church

Now flip the card over (or use a fresh one) and write “I love” at the top. Do the same thing with love instead of belief.

Ask:

  • Which was the hardest belief for you to write? (Most groups say the Holy Spirit.)
  • Was it easier to name what you believe about God, or what you love about God? Why?
  • Is there a connection between what you believe about someone and what you love about someone? Explain.

Pray:

Thank you for all the faithful people who have come before us. Help us to learn more about you. Where we have questions, give us patience and a willingness to listen for answers. Where we are confident, give us patience and a willingness to share our faith in new ways. Give us the willingness to listen to others and to speak honestly. Amen.

Read — 2 Timothy 1

Say:

First and Second Timothy and Titus are called the Pastoral Letters (or Pastoral Epistles) in the New Testament. The Apostle Paul — or another church leader writing in Paul’s name — sent these letters to pastors with advice about how to face the challenges of standing firm in the faith.

Distribute Bibles. Invite volunteers to read Acts 16:1–2 and 1 Thessalonians 3:2, 5–6 to learn a bit about Timothy.

Have participants turn to 2 Timothy 1. Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 1–14.

Ask:

  • What is the problem Paul is addressing in the letter?
  • Why do you think Paul mentions Timothy’s mother and grandmother? (v.5)
  • What is the sound teaching Paul is referring to? Why is it important that Christians “hold to the standard of sound teaching” (v.13)?
  • Which of Paul’s words to Timothy do you find most encouraging? Why?

To help participants understand v.6, ask a volunteer to find and read aloud Acts 13:2–3. Point out that the Spirit was given to Paul through the laying on of hands, and Paul gave this gift to Timothy.

Have participants read aloud 2 Timothy 1:9–10 in unison. Tell them scholars think these verses quote an early Christian creed — words of faith that early Christians recited in worship.

Ask: What word or phrase from these verses grabs your attention the most? Why?

Paraphrase Exercise

Distribute copies of Resource 8a (CEB + The Message). Ask volunteers to read the verses aloud.

Ask each participant to write a paraphrase of 2 Timothy 1:9–10 (BIG Journal p.74) as either an “I believe” or “I love” statement. Ask volunteers to read their paraphrases to the group.

Ask:

  • What important things do we learn about what Christians believe from these verses?
  • How might speaking creeds together in worship encourage Christians?

The Apostles’ Creed

Say:

A creed helps us describe what we affirm as Christians. The word creed has the connotation of “I give my heart” and is often translated as “I believe.”

The Apostles’ Creed was developed over a few hundred years after Jesus lived. It’s based on Scripture and the understanding and experience of the earliest Christians.

Parallel example:

Centuries after the Constitution was written, Martin Luther King Jr. argued for abolishing segregation based on the Declaration of Independence (all men are created equal). He appealed to a document everyone held to be authoritative in how this country should run. The Apostles’ Creed is our version of that — a document the church has agreed names what it stands on.

Read the Apostles’ Creed aloud (BIG Journal p.72–73). A printable copy: https://www.pcabookstore.com/samples/11589.pdf

Ask:

  • Why is it important to notice that the creed begins with the words “I believe in…”?

Close

Say:

We’ll revisit the Apostles’ Creed and learn more about our own worship service next week.

Close in prayer.

item.title: Session 8B · Teaching
item.description:
Part B of Session 8 · Teaching.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules
  • Note cards + pens
  • Apostles’ Creed matching cards (kept in the confirmation/retreat room). Enough to pair students; otherwise two-sided copies cut from the teacher’s guide. Collect these in bunches afterward for next year.
  • Smartphones or tablets for students to look up symbols
  • Copies of your church’s worship bulletin (one per student)
  • Whiteboard + markers

Write the big question on the board again:

WILL YOU DEVOTE YOURSELF TO THE CHURCH’S TEACHING?

Opener

Confirmation ground rules.

Say:

Back to the Apostles’ Creed — the summary of our faith that has been the central expression of the church’s teaching from the beginning.

Read the creed aloud together.

Symbols of the Creed

Pass out the symbol cards. Have the group research the meaning of the symbols on their phones or tablets.

Challenge: draw symbols for the phrases that aren’t on the cards:

  • “Maker of heaven and earth”
  • “He descended into hell”
  • “The quick and the dead”
  • “The communion of saints”

Add these to the symbol cards.

Consider putting them in pairs. Have them try to put the creed in order just looking at the symbols.

The Cloud of Witnesses

Say:

The phrase “the communion of saints” in the Apostles’ Creed refers to the relationships among Christians past, present, and future. The New Testament letter called Hebrews describes this communion as a “cloud of witnesses.” The cloud refers to all the saints who have gone before us as our encouragers in the faith — like our great forebears Abraham and Sarah, or the ones who are great only for us, like our parents and teachers in the faith.

Invite a volunteer to read aloud Hebrews 12:1–2.

Ask:

  • What images of racing did you notice?
    • The “cloud of witnesses” — the runners who have already finished the race and are cheering on those still competing, identified by name in Hebrews 11.
    • “Lay aside every weight” — runners of that time ran with little or no clothing, in order to run faster.
    • The runner keeping their eyes fixed on the goal, which is Jesus Christ.
  • What words of encouragement did you hear?
  • Why might the people who first heard this Scripture have needed words of encouragement?
  • Why do we need encouragement?

Worship as Trained Discipleship

Hand out copies of your church’s worship service bulletin. Write the main components on the board.

Say:

Sunday worship is like a gymnasium — it’s meant to train and teach us to be disciples of Christ throughout the week.

Ask:

  • What is your favorite part?
  • Which parts of the service require responses from us?
  • What, if anything, would you change in our worship so that sacrificially following Jesus was emphasized?

Work through the components of the service and talk about what they’re teaching us — what they’re inviting us into.

A useful frame: Isaiah 6. The structure of Christian worship grows out of Isaiah’s vision of the throne room.

Isaiah 6 as the Shape of Worship

Isaiah 6:1 — “In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord sitting on a throne, high and lofty, and the hem of his robe filled the temple.”

Worship begins with God.

v.2–3 — “Seraphs were in attendance above him; each had six wings… And one called to another and said, ‘Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory.’”

The call to worship — affirming God’s goodness and holiness.

v.4–5 — “The pivots on the thresholds shook at the voices of those who called, and the house filled with smoke. And I said, ‘Woe is me! I am lost, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I live among a people of unclean lips, yet my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts!’”

Confession — in response to God’s holiness and our sin.

v.6–7 — “Then one of the seraphs flew to me, holding a live coal… The seraph touched my mouth with it and said, ‘Now that this has touched your lips, your guilt has departed and your sin is blotted out.’”

Assurance of pardon.

v.8a — “Then I heard the voice of the Lord saying, ‘Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?’”

Sermon / Scripture — the word of God.

v.8b — “And I said, ‘Here am I; send me!’”

Response / offering — sending.

Close

Read the Apostles’ Creed as a closing and pray:

Holy God, we praise you for the example of the heroes of faith who have run this race before us. Give us strength and perseverance, and help us fix our eyes on Jesus, in whose name we pray. Amen.

Optional — Where Did the Bible Come From?

If you have extra time or the class has questions about scripture, the source material includes an extended teaching on why we trust the Bible, including:

  • How the canon of the New Testament was formed (through the fourth century)
  • Apostolic authority and its transmission through the bishops
  • Why the “telephone game” argument against Scripture is weak — the care taken by scribes, the volume of manuscripts, the precision vs. other ancient texts
  • Why the “Christianity was invented to keep people in line” argument is historically inaccurate — Pliny the Younger’s 113 AD letter to Trajan describing execution of Christians, and why early Christians had nothing to gain from inventing the faith

Optional — The Barmen Declaration and the Confession of Belhar

If your group has older or more curious confirmands, split into two groups (one teacher each). One goes over the Barmen Declaration (Nazi Germany, 1934); the other goes over the Confession of Belhar (apartheid South Africa, 1986). The Confession of 1967 (civil rights, nuclear war) can substitute or supplement.

Hand out copies of Resource 8f. Let them use phones to look up the historical background.

Discuss:

  • What is required of a church to take a stand in times of crisis?
  • How can a faith statement that takes a stand create change for good in the world?
  • For whom or what would you take a stand in the middle of the cafeteria? In the middle of the congregation?
  • Against what evil or injustice in the world would you write a faith statement?

item.title: Session 9A · Fellowship
item.description:
Part A of Session 9 · Fellowship.

Before class

  • Confirmation ground rules; choose someone to pray
  • Paper and pens for every student
  • Bibles

Opener — Koinonia

Ask: Can anyone guess what this Greek word means?

Koinonia — Fellowship.

Big question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s fellowship?

Say:

Koinonia expresses the idea of sharing in a common bond. Action is always included in its meaning — it implies contribution, sharing, and participation. Fellowship is not just being together; it’s doing together.

Doing life together. Repeat after me: Doing life together.

The Priesthood of All Believers

Say:

We want to begin by talking about what the Presbyterian church calls the priesthood of all believers.

Ask: Any guesses as to what this means?

Read — 1 Peter 2:4–5:

Come to him, a living stone, though rejected by mortals yet chosen and precious in God’s sight, and like living stones, let yourselves be built into a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.

Say:

The priesthood of all believers asserts that all humans have access to God through Christ, the true high priest, and thus do not need a priestly mediator.

Priests acted as the middle person between us and God. Priests of this nature are still used in the Catholic and Orthodox traditions, but when the notion of the priesthood of all believers was introduced in the Reformation, it made the claim that all Christians were equal.

The priesthood of all believers also signifies that all members of the body of Christ can speak and contribute to the life of the church.

So, as we are called to fellowship — koinonia, doing life together — it’s a fellowship as the priesthood of all believers, where we all have equal access to God.

The Two Marks of Koinonia: Gathering and Love

Say:

The fellowship — koinonia — of the church is ultimately marked by two things: gathering and love.

First — Gathering

Ask a volunteer to read Hebrews 10:24–25:

And let us consider how we may spur one another on toward love and good deeds, not giving up meeting together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging one another — and all the more as you see the Day approaching.

Answer in your group, or as a family:

  1. What is one of your favorite memories gathering at a church function?
  2. In what ways can your family gather with the people of God? (Church on Sunday, dinner / prayer with other Christian families, events like Pancake Dinner, mission trips, etc.)
  3. Why might you need to encourage one another when it comes to continuing to gather?
  4. What are some spaces you want to commit to gathering in after this confirmation class is over? (Church, Sunday School, youth group, retreats, etc.)

Second — Love

Introduce the reading:

This story takes place while Jesus and his disciples are eating the Passover dinner — what people sometimes call the Last Supper. In Jesus’ day, most people traveled on foot wearing sandals. Their feet quickly got very dusty. In well-to-do households, a servant would wash a visitor’s feet when they entered. This job was considered the lowliest of all the servant tasks. In this encounter, Jesus offers the disciples a vivid example of how they are to be a loving community based on his example.

Read John 13:3–5, 14–15:

Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going to God, got up from the table, took off his outer robe, and tied a towel around himself. Then he poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples’ feet and to wipe them with the towel that was tied around him… “So if I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another’s feet. For I have set you an example, that you also should do as I have done to you.”

Ask (in small groups if you have time; otherwise big group):
